subject: do chips have to be spent first?

posts.body:
When paying for new cards, and I have more than tje cost of one gem color when adding my development cards and my chip tokens, do I have to spend out the chip tokens first?

This is how we played in order to keep chips available and push people to play for the higher value cards.  And we find this works very well.

But we wonder what is the official rule.  

To clarify, an example wpuld be that the card I want requires 5 red gems.  I have 3 tokens and 4 development cards.  

Do I have to spend, return, the 3 red tokens first, and then not fully utilize the development cards?  Or do I max out the development cards and only spend 1 token, thereby keeping two in my reserve?

TIA
Tom

The cards work as discounts or virtual currency.  So if a card costs five and you have 4 cards, it costs you one gem.

Edit Beaten by a literal second.
What part of the rulebook made you think that this could be the way of play?

2 things.  First the rules didn't explicitly staye that amd we got the impression that chip hoarding is a negative factor oft discussed, so inentives to keep chip liquidity made sense.

Second is the card symbolism.  It is notable that the noble cards use rectangles to show their costs, ie. Use cards to meet tje requirement, while cards showed circle for their cost, which we took to imply use chips primarily.  

We'll try the proper rules play.  But I could see us returning to the system we used as play has. Even good, balanced, and never an issue with chip hoarding as they must go back into the pool each time a buy is made.
There is no answer. In all of my games, we use your technique
There is no answer. In all of my games, we use your technique

tommatty wrote:

2 things.  First the rules didn't explicitly staye that amd we got the impression that chip hoarding is a negative factor oft discussed, so inentives to keep chip liquidity made sense.

Chip hoarding is *not* a negative.  It is an important strategy to help keep your opponent's buying power in check.

iloveashesreborn wrote:

There is no answer. In all of my games, we use your technique

iloveashesreborn wrote:

There is no answer. In all of my games, we use your technique

The rulebook does indeed cover this.
The rulebook does indeed cover this.

It says you *must* use your card discounts first, before spending any chips:

Splendor Rules, Pg. 4 wrote:

The bonuses a player has from development cards acquired on previous turns provide discounts on the purchase of new cards.  Each bonus of a given color is equal to a token of that color.

Thus, if a player has 2 blue bonuses and wants to purchase a card which costs 2 blue tokens and 1 green token, the player must only spend 1 green token.

(bold emphasis mine).  It does not say you *may* spend two blue tokens and one green token if you have them, even if you have two blue cards.  It says you *must* only spend the one green token.  And, by association, that also means you *must* apply the two blue discount from your two blue cards.

That said, it's your game.  If you want to house rule it that way, then go ahead.  Just know that your method is indeed a house rule, and is not the official, nor intended, way of play according to the rulebook.

Cheers!

That said, it's your game.  If you want to house rule it that way, then go ahead.  Just know that your method is indeed a house rule, and is not the official, nor intended, way of play according to the rulebook.

This.

Bonuses give you a discount. You aren’t “spending” them, they just reduce the price you must pay.
Thanks everyone for answering this.  It is clear that development cards can/ perhaps must be utilized first.  We're going to play this way from now on and.  Should be an interesting comparison.

tommatty wrote:

Thanks everyone for answering this.  It is clear that development cards can/ perhaps must be utilized first.  We're going to play this way from now on and.  Should be an interesting comparison.

Chip hoarding is part of the game in a way. It can force players to start buying cards when the chips they want are not available.

CavemanLogic wrote:

It says you *must* use your card discounts first, before spending any chips:

Splendor Rules, Pg. 4 wrote:

The bonuses a player has from development cards acquired on previous turns provide discounts on the purchase of new cards.  Each bonus of a given color is equal to a token of that color.

Thus, if a player has 2 blue bonuses and wants to purchase a card which costs 2 blue tokens and 1 green token, the player must only spend 1 green token.

(bold emphasis mine).  It does not say you *may* spend two blue tokens and one green token if you have them, even if you have two blue cards.  It says you *must* only spend the one green token.  And, by association, that also means you *must* apply the two blue discount from your two blue cards.

That said, it's your game.  If you want to house rule it that way, then go ahead.  Just know that your method is indeed a house rule, and is not the official, nor intended, way of play according to the rulebook.

Cheers!

I don’t think you’re reading “must” quite correctly there. Often when translating from German to English, there’s some confusion between “must” and “have to”. (“Musst nicht” in German is often mistranslated “must not” but actually means “do not have to”.)

In this case I think it means you need to spend only one green token. If a player wanted to spend more tokens in that case I don’t think the rules are intended to disallow it. But I can’t imagine why anyone would want to!

That said, it's your game.  If you want to house rule it that way, then go ahead.  Just know that your method is indeed a house rule, and is not the official, nor intended, way of play according to the rulebook.

Aye, good play is to choke the supply so your opponent is screwed. So taking the full discount and holding onto your chips is always the better option.
